nativeRange.habitat: Shallow clear coastal and outer reef crests with rich coral growth, typically to about 15 m depth; depth range 1–28 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on marine and blue-green algae (Spirulina, Chlorella) growing on live rock. Has been documented accepting frozen and dried algae-based foods, and may opportunistically take small meaty items such as brine shrimp or mysid shrimp. Specimens that lack sufficient algae in the aquarium tend to decline; supplemental algae-based foods are generally advised.

avoidWith: fish that may intimidate small perching species

summary: A small, peaceful herbivorous blenny from Indo-Pacific reefs, generally considered easy to keep in reef aquariums with ample live rock for grazing and perching. Generally reef-safe, though isolated SPS coral nipping has been reported. Requires a tightly sealed lid as these fish tend to jump.
careNotes:
The Linear Blenny (Ecsenius lineatus) is generally regarded as a suitable choice for beginners to reef-keeping. It tends to spend most of its time perching on or darting between rocks and rubble, grazing on algae growing on live rock surfaces. A tight-fitting lid or sealed hood is strongly advised — this species is commonly described as a notorious escape artist.

These fish have been reported to occasionally nip at the polyps or bases of SPS corals, particularly Acropora and Montipora, especially in lightly stocked or coral-dominated systems. Adding one to an established SPS reef is generally done with caution.

Conspecific aggression is notable: generally advised to keep only one per aquarium unless a mated pair can be established. Multiple males or similarly colored blennies in the same tank tend to lead to persistent chasing.

This species should not be housed with aggressive tankmates that may bully it into permanent hiding, which can lead to starvation. Diet is primarily algae-based; specimens that lack sufficient natural algae in the aquarium have been known to slowly waste away without supplemental algae foods.

Temperature sources show some spread: hobby sources typically recommend 72–78°F (22–26°C) while FishBase wild data records 24.6–29.3°C; a practical range of approximately 23–28°C is commonly cited across sources.

FishBase notes reproduction involves distinct pairing with demersal, adhesive eggs attached to substrate — breeding in captivity has not been widely documented.
breedingNotes: FishBase records this species as oviparous with distinct pairing behavior; eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to substrate via a filamentous pad. Breeding in the aquarium hobby has not been widely documented, and detailed captive-breeding protocols are not available in the sources consulted.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (confirmed 9.0 cm TL), native range (Yemen to NW Australia, Philippines, Japan), temperature range (24.6–29.3°C wild data). FishBase temperature max is higher than hobby sources (25.6–26.7°C); averaged across sources yields 23–28°C practical range.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–80°F / 22–26.7°C), tank size (30 gal), reef-safe caveats (SPS coral nipping), and conspecific aggression. Consistent with primary source.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Confirmed escape risk (aquarium must be covered), diet notes (small crustaceans opportunistically taken), substrate preference (rubble and gravel). Also noted this species lacks teeth — food must be appropriately sized.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; demersal adhesive eggs. Captive breeding not widely documented in the hobby.
